title: Bergamot and vetiver reed diffuser
subtitle: A reed diffuser with citrus brightness and a deep, smoky base
excerpt: A room fragrance diffuser made with dipropylene glycol, bergamot essential oil, and vetiver essential oil. The DPG carrier wicks the fragrance blend up through the reeds and disperses it slowly into the room. Makes one 100 ml diffuser.
sourceNotes: Dipropylene glycol (DPG) as a fragrance carrier for reed diffusers: standard diffuser base, documented in fragrance industry technical literature. DPG is odourless, has a low vapour pressure that allows slow, steady fragrance release, and is compatible with essential oils and fragrance oils. Bergamot and vetiver essential oil safety at room-diffuser concentrations: no dermal contact, so concentration limits are not directly applicable; ventilated room use is standard.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 reed diffuser (~100 ml, approximately 4 to 6 weeks of diffusion)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Total essential oil content stays at 15 to 20% of formula weight for a good room throw.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 1 hour for the reeds to fully absorb before judging the throw. Flip the reeds to refresh the diffusion every 2 to 3 days.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: A sealed diffuser bottle keeps 6 months. Once opened and the reeds inserted, the diffuser will last 4 to 6 weeks in a normal-sized room before the fragrance is depleted. The throw diminishes before the bottle is fully empty; flipping the reeds extends it.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which an essential oil can ignite near a flame. Bergamot and vetiver both have flash points well above room temperature, making them safe for reed diffuser use.

content.text: Reed diffusers release fragrance by capillary action: the reeds absorb the liquid fragrance mixture and carry it to the surface, where it evaporates into the air. Dipropylene glycol (DPG) is the standard carrier for this application. It is odourless, has a very low vapour pressure, and draws the fragrance oil up the reed shaft at a steady rate rather than releasing a sudden burst. An alcohol-based carrier (isopropyl alcohol) releases faster and more intensely but depletes within a week; DPG is slower and more sustained, giving 4 to 6 weeks from a 100 ml bottle. The fragrance load here is 17% of formula weight (15 ml essential oil to 85 ml DPG), within the standard 15 to 20% range for a good room throw. Both bergamot and vetiver essential oils have
values well above room temperature, making them safe for this application.

Measure the DPG into the amber glass bottle. Add both essential oils. Fit the cap and tilt gently to combine. Insert the diffuser reeds and allow 1 hour for the reeds to fully absorb before judging the fragrance throw. Flip all the reeds (wet end up, dry end in the liquid) every 2 to 3 days to refresh the diffusion.

Place the diffuser on a stable surface where it will not be knocked over; the DPG and essential oil blend will stain fabric and some surfaces. Keep away from children and pets; essential oils at room-diffuser concentrations are not intended for ingestion. A warm position (near (but not touching) a radiator) increases evaporation and throw; a cool position slows it and extends the life of the diffuser. Avoid positioning directly in a draught, which dissipates the fragrance before it can disperse evenly.
An unsealed diffuser in use depletes in 4 to 6 weeks. A sealed bottle not yet opened keeps 6 months. The bergamot top note will fade before the vetiver base over the active life of the diffuser.
